lines.text: A letter that was never sent — written, sealed, addressed, and placed here by someone who did not trust the postal systems of their time. Their caution proved correct. The dungeon is more reliable.
The cold in this level is the specific cold of winter correspondence — the cold of rooms where letters were written by candlelight because the hearth had not yet been lit, or had already gone out.
The exile's archive was built for those who were forced to carry their culture in letters rather than in land. The dungeon preserved what they preserved, for the same reasons.
Birch bark and iron — the oldest documents in this level predate paper in this region. They are not fragile. They are older than the concept of fragility in this context.
The cedar trunk in the corner is locked. The lock is simple — the key was lost with the family it belonged to. The dungeon has not forced it. It considers the lock part of the archive.
The alphabet was designed to carry a specific kind of sound — sounds that other writing systems approximated poorly. The letters in this level are the sounds themselves, given a shape to live in.
Multiple scripts on the same document — old and new alphabets, the same text, the same content, the same meaning carried by different shapes. The dungeon preserved both versions.
The smell of old correspondence — paper and ink and the faint residue of the wax that sealed documents that no longer exist. The dungeon maintains the smell for atmospheric continuity.
The letters here crossed borders that no longer exist. The dungeon maps the old borders too — keeps the routes the letters traveled, the checkpoints they passed through.
Languages survive when communities carry them — in exile, underground, in private correspondence. What survives on these walls survived that way.
